data.title: BMEZ: This Healthcare Fund Is Paying Out More Than It Earns
data.description:
Overview

BlackRock Health Sciences Term Trust (NYSE:BMEZ) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide a diverse exposure to high quality healthcare businesses. As the name implies, BMEZ is a termed fund with a maturity that was set for twelve years, which means that the fund has an estimated liquidation in 2032. The fund has an inception dating back to 2020 and has a management fee of 1.25%. However, the fund puts an emphasis on income generation, which makes it an attractive choice for income investors seeking a high yield.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that BMEZ's share price has declined by about 12.3%. The overall healthcare sector has lagged behind other industries, so this price decline comes as no surprise. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, BMEZ has a total return of only 1.5% over the same period. One of the most attractive aspects to the fund is the starting dividend yield of 14.2% and monthly distributions. However, the recent reporting indicates that the fund's performance has not been sufficient to fully support the distributions that are paid. Therefore, the overall sustainability of the distribution is questionable at this point.

Due to the healthcare sector's ongoing weakness, BMEZ trades at an attractive discount to NAV valuation. For investors that are optimistic about the outlook of healthcare, this could present an attractive opportunity for investors to start accumulating shares. However, there are certainly some risks and vulnerabilities of the fund in the short-term. For instance, the distributions may continue to get reduced if the internal performance doesn't improve. Additionally, the fund includes option writing as a part of the strategy, which can limit the overall total return potential of the fund. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that BMEZ implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, BMEZ has total managed assets of $958M that are spread across 157 different positions. The fund aims to invest at least 80% of its assets into equity securities that operate within the health science sub-sector of healthcare. Although the fund is most heavily focused on companies with primary operations in the US, BMEZ also provides a bit of global exposure as follows:

  • United States: 81.4%
  • Switzerland: 6.7%
  • China: 3.2%
  • United Kingdom: 2.2%
  • Germany: 2.2%
  • Denmark: 1.8%
  • Japan: 1.0%
  • Israel: 0.9%
  • Belgium: 0.7%

As we can see below, the fund is most heavily concentrated on assets that are Pharma, Biotech, & Life Science related, which makes up 59.3% of the fund. This is followed by exposure to Health Care Equipment & Services and Tech Hardware investments, which account for 37.7% and 2.24% of assets respectively. However, investors should expect the top sector to have the greatest impact on BMEZ's daily price movement and total return potential.

When looking at the top holdings, we can see many recognizable large-cap companies. For instance, Alnylam Pharmaceuticals (ALNY) is the largest position at 4.9% of assets. There are also varying weights to other large-cap companies like Veeva Systems (VEEV), Dexcom (DXCM), and Mettler-Toledo International (MTD). BMEZ also provides some private investment exposure with positions like 'Project Picasso'. After some digging through different documents, I was able to locate that Project Picasso is actually related to a company called PsiQuantum.

As a way to generate additional income from its holdings, BMEZ implements an option writing strategy. BMEZ can write options against its own holdings or against the Nasdaq Biotechnology Index. The inclusion of an option strategy means that there's a high probability that BMEZ will underperform many of its own holdings over a longer time horizon. BMEZ can collect option premiums from its positions, which it uses to help fund the distributions to shareholders. The option strategy is essentially trading the growth potential for current income.

Just to demonstrate, I pulled in a price comparison of BMEZ against many of its own holdings. As we can see below, BMEZ's share price has declined by a little more than 30% since its inception. However, many of its own holdings have experienced a high level of growth over the same period, with ALNY appreciating more than 238% over the same time frame.

Valuation

Since BMEZ operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual underlying value of its assets. As a result, BMEZ currently trades at a discount to NAV of about 7%. However, BMEZ has traded at an average discount to NAV of approximately 12.2% over the last three year period, which may indicate that current valuation isn't as attractive at this time. Even looking back over the fund's entire history below, we can see that the current discount to NAV pricing sits at the higher end of the fund's range since inception.

To get better clarity into the fund's internal performance, I reviewed the most recent annual report for 2024. Net investment income amounted to $0.04 per share, while net realized gains were $0.17 per share. Looking back on prior years, the level of net investment income has consistently been negative, indicating a weakness in the fund's ability to generate income. There aren't many holdings within BMEZ that put an emphasis on dividend growth for shareholders, so this poor net investment income performance isn't really a surprise. However, this weakness makes the fund heavily reliant on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth and support distributions.

The only issue is that net realized gains are also very inconsistent and generally reliant on positive market conditions to fuel growth. Option premiums that are collected are typically included as part of net realized gains as well. What's more important is that positive market momentum allows BMEZ to sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. As you can imagine, it can be difficult for a fund to capture positive gains if market indexes are declining and the healthcare sector has trended downward over recent years. This inconsistent performance has resulted in the fund's NAV being significantly reduced since the fund's inception. For reference, here are the fund's year-end NAV amounts since inception:

  • 2020: $30.73 per share
  • 2021: $26.47 per share
  • 2022: $18.76 per share
  • 2023: $17.91 per share
  • 2024: $16.43 per share

The fund's dependence on positive market conditions makes it a very difficult position to recommend for long term income investors. For a fund that puts an emphasis on income generation, I would love to see the fund's net investment income levels improve in the future. Improved net investment income levels would take away from the fund's reliance on positive market conditions and also provide better price and NAV stability for investors. I imagine that an easy way to achieve this would be to include some income securities as a way to boost the level of cash the fund collects.

Risk Profile

One of the most interesting risks for the fund has been the higher interest rate environment. Despite BMEZ not utilizing any leverage, the fund has demonstrated an inverse relationship to the federal funds rate. Shortly after the fund's launch, the federal funds rate was cut to near zero levels as a way to provide stimulus in response to the pandemic. This made it very cost efficient for businesses to borrow capital as a way to fuel operational growth. Healthcare and Life Science companies are generally reliant on access to cheap debt financing as a way to fuel the research and development of new products. The lower interest rate environment caused the fund's share price to rapidly increase throughout 2020 and 2021.

Conversely, BMEZ's share price retracted just as quickly when the federal funds rate was increased to its highest level in a decade. The higher cost of borrowing made it a lot harder for businesses to effectively use leverage as a way to fuel growth. A higher interest rate means that a greater percentage of earnings needed to go towards interest payments on debt. This higher interest rate environment has kept BMEZ's share price suppressed since 2022 and the fund has struggled to regain any positive momentum since then. Therefore, there's a risk that higher interest rates keep the healthcare sector suppressed.

For instance, the Fed has chosen to keep interest rates unchanged in their latest meeting, as they continue to monitor macroeconomic data like inflation, unemployment rates, and consumer spending levels. Additionally, the full impact of President Trump's tariffs are still to be determined, so there's a risk that interest rate cuts will be pushed back until 2026, as I believe the Fed wants to avoid cutting rates too early. Therefore, investors must consider the interest rate risk before initiating a position in BMEZ.

Additionally, the fund's option strategy means that it may not be able to participate in the upside momentum of the healthcare sector. The growth of BMEZ is essentially capped at whatever the selected strike price is. So when the healthcare sector eventually recovers, I believe that BMEZ may get outpaced by more traditional ETFs, such as the Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLV). Just as an example, I pulled in a total return comparison between the two funds and we can see that XLV outpaces BMEZ, despite XLV having a much lower dividend yield of only 1.8%.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1672 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 14.2%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $2.0064 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, the fund has had trouble generating earnings in excess of this payout amount. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts going back to inception:

  • 2020: $11.63 per share
  • 2021: ($2.55) per share
  • 2022: ($5.97) per share
  • 2023: $0.73 per share
  • 2024: $0.21 per share.

As we can see, BMEZ was only able to generate a sufficient level of earnings back in 2020, which was when interest rates were low. The fact that the fund is now paying out more than it actually earns means that I have concerns about the distribution sustainability going forward. We can see below that the payouts were reduced in 2023 due to weaker earnings. Based on the earnings for 2024, I believe that the fund's distribution policy is too generous and a cut is needed. A lower distribution would help relieve the pressure of the fund needed to generate a high level of earnings and it would better preserve the health of the NAV during these unfavorable market conditions.

It's also worth noting that the fund is heavily reliant on return of capital distributions to pay the distributions. Return of capital distributions are typically used when the fund isn't generating enough income to support payouts. While these distributions can be more tax-efficient, they exacerbate the issue of a declining NAV. Therefore, it becomes difficult to recommend a position in BMEZ for income investors at this time.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that BMEZ does not offer a compelling value proposition for investors at this time. Despite the fund trading at a sizable discount to NAV valuation, the internal performance needs to be substantially improved. Net investment income levels need to be improved and net realized gains have been inconsistent. This poor performance also leads to a questionable dividend sustainability for income investors, as I believe that BMEZ would benefit from a cut. The reliance on return of capital also contributes to the fund's declining NAV over time. For these reasons, I believe that BMEZ is a sell at this time.

data.title: I'm Buying This Market Dip With Leverage
data.description:
In my previous article on the broader markets, I projected that the S&P 500 (SP500) would hit all-time highs around mid-to-late summer. Well, it seems that I was right.

It's been a long time since I last bought stocks, ETFs, or call options on red days, so being able to do so on August 1 was a welcome opportunity, following the July nonfarm payrolls report and Trump's latest update on reciprocal tariffs.

In this article, I discuss why I believe an increase in unemployment in the US is extremely bullish for my bull case on the broader markets. As expected by looking at articles on SA, most analysts have tempered down their optimism after the recent jobs report came in weaker than previously estimated. Not in my case. In fact, I loaded the truck, investing the last of the cash I had remaining.

That said, risks remain, as tariffs are back in the spotlight. However, I believe the optimism we have seen since the April lows is intact, as Trump has pushed back (once again, what a surprise!) the implementation of the reciprocal tariffs, conveniently stating that US customs needed extra time to integrate the new reciprocal rates into the Harmonized Tariff Schedule. Of course, I believe this extra week is to allow a brief window to hash out further exceptions or tailored carve-outs that might otherwise have lapsed under an unmovable August 1 start date.

Overall, I expect this dip to be a good buying opportunity for funds that missed the bull run and avoided buying on green days. Therefore, I believe the dip will be short-lived.

I rate the broader market in the US as a strong buy, reiterating my previous thesis. This time, I don't have any price targets in mind due to the lack of historical data in the chart. That said, high $600s is not an unreasonable target before year-end.

Great News, Unemployment Is Ticking Up

I encourage readers to ask themselves the following question and provide their thoughts in the comments section of this article: For large enterprises in the US, what is the single biggest upside from adopting AI into their daily operations?

I have a very strong conviction in my answer: to rightsize their headcount. This is the polite way to say: to maximize their bottom line by aggressively laying off unnecessary employees. You aren't convinced? Fair enough. Take a look at the following statements made by CEOs of US public companies.

Over the last 15 years or so, we went from 300,000 people to 212,000 people... We just got to keep working that down. - Brian Moynihan, Bank of America.

The best leaders get the most done with the least number of resources required to do the job. - Andy Jassy, Amazon.

Flatter is faster. - Marie Myers, Hewlett Packard Enterprise

We’re going to launch 10 products… The challenge I have for our team is: How do we launch those 10 products without adding head count? - Stéphane Bancel, Moderna

At the margin, we’re asking people to resist head count growth where possible and increase their focus on efficiency. - Jeremy Barnum, JPMorgan Chase

And, my favorite one (for fellow developers and engineers):

We, at Meta as well as the other companies working on this, are going to have an AI that can effectively be sort of a mid-level engineer at your company. Over time we’ll get to the point where a lot of the code in our apps … is actually going to be built by AI engineers instead of people engineers. - Mark Zuckerberg, META

But don't worry, Jensen has the answer to this problem:

You’re not going to lose your job to an AI, but you’re going to lose your job to someone who uses AI. - Jensen Huang, Nvidia

Back in the day, I'm talking about the 2000s, headcount was one of the best metrics to gauge for revenue growth. If you suddenly stopped hiring, it meant that you were in big trouble.

The story of Cisco Systems back in 2001 is a great example. After more than 40 straight quarters of growth, Cisco reported its first negative quarter in Q3 2021. After a few weeks of downplaying the slowdown, the company announced a hiring freeze. Within days of that freeze, Cisco laid off 8,500 employees, wrote off $2.2 billion of excess inventory, and saw its share price collapse from $82 to $13.63 over the next year... Yikes!

I have a very strong conviction that in today's landscape, a decline in headcount is highly welcomed by investors in both the public and private markets. In fact, an increase in headcount is a clear indication that management is unable to leverage AI to their advantage. Of course, there are exceptions for companies that require a physical workforce (nurses, oil rig workers, truck drivers, clinics, etc.), at least, until humanoids become mainstream in the next few decades.

You don't believe me, again? Fair enough.

One of the best examples that comes to my mind is AppLovin. Take a look at what happened to revenue growth back in 2021/2022, when the company ramped up its headcount, vs. what happened in 2024, when they rightsized their organization.

Now that I've made my point, I'd like to be direct. Here is one of the main pillars of my bull case:

A decline in headcount, coupled with an increase in unemployment in the US is extremely bullish for US shareholders.

There are two main reasons.

The first one, well, I already said it above. For most companies (especially those in the technology sector), a decline in headcount could be an indication of cost efficiency due to the adoption of AI agents. This efficiency can lead to an improvement in the bottom line, and in some cases, even in the top line, if the company really knows how to leverage AI to its advantage. This is great news for shareholders and bad news for employees (particularly, entry-level ones).

Second, and this is key, an increase in unemployment is extremely bullish from an interest rate perspective. By looking at the current economic data, the Fed has little to no reason to cut interest rates this year. My prediction is that we will have only one rate cut this year. This is against the consensus, which is leaning for two rate cuts by the January 2026 meeting, as shown in the chart below.

However, in the long term, I do believe that unemployment in the US will pick up. And that's when I predict that interest rates will come down drastically. Maybe not to pandemic levels, but likely down by several percentage points from today's range.

It's difficult to know when this rate cut is going to happen, although I propose a very simple indicator: the growth rate of Palantir's US commercial segment. Why?

This segment is driven by enterprises adopting the AIP platform, in my opinion, the benchmark for building and integrating AI agents into an organization's workflow. Below is a chart representing the revenue growth of Palantir's US commercial segment in the US.

Now, before you go on and build an AI agent to do your work at your day job, I warn you that Palantir's AIP is not available for consumers.

Back to my point, as long as this segment is growing, I can say quite confidently that enterprise adoption of AI in the US is ticking up. Therefore, one can only expect a decline in headcount once the AI agents are mature enough and, therefore, an increase in unemployment, as the US job market will tighten. That increase in unemployment will most likely pressure the FED to cut rates. Both of these effects (rate cuts + cost efficiencies) are positive for shareholders of US equities.

Risks

Looking at the near term, tariffs are once again in the spotlight. I considered including below a very interesting chart by Reuters.

The trend above looks favorable, with significant downward revisions from the previously known tariff rates. To keep things clear, I considered including below the import share and the tariff rate for the top 5 trading partners with the US.

The one tariff that caught the market's attention was the hike in the baseline tariff on Canadian goods from 25% to 35%, effective August 1, 2025. Interestingly, Canada is the only trading partner whose rate has been made effective on August 1. Unsurprisingly (to me), the implementation of these reciprocal tariffs was officially pushed back by one week (now August 7, 2025) in order to give US customs extra time to integrate the new rates into their systems.

Conclusion

I cannot help but wonder if the "final" rates we will see next week, particularly for the top 5 trading partners (except China, as the deadline is August 12), will be lower than what we saw today.

If these rates are the same, there is a good chance for another selloff, which I believe could temper some of the recent optimism. Now, is this selloff enough to take profits and stay uninvested? I argue the answer is no, but readers need to remember that I am a high-risk speculator. My appetite for risk (and returns) is abnormal, to say the least, and therefore not suitable for most investors.

As for the unemployment rate, I am not sweating a drop to see the metric ticking up. In fact, I will start to sweat if I see this key metric going down. I certainly believe that large enterprises need to optimize costs by laying off employees (who do nothing productive most of the day). If unemployment goes up, the FED will be forced to cut down interest rates. Both effects are highly favorable for investors (not so much for entry-level employees and the general public).

Overall, I believe the upside is larger than the downside. That's why I reiterate my strong buy rating.

data.title: Comstock Resources: Production Declines Despite $1 Billion In Capex
data.description:
Comstock Resources (NYSE:CRK) lowered its production guidance for 2025 by approximately 6% while keeping its capex budget unchanged. This results in a projection that it could end up with close to $150 million in full-year cash burn now.

Comstock still is expected to have plenty of liquidity, but I've reduced its estimated value by $1 to approximately $13.50 per share now in a long-term (after 2026) $3.75 NYMEX natural gas environment.

This is due to Comstock's weaker production results and the impact of lower 2025 and 2026 natural gas strip prices on its projected near-term free cash flow.

I thought Comstock was very overvalued before, but I believe it is only moderately overvalued (for a long-term $3.75 NYMEX natural gas scenario) now that its shares are down close to 50% from their highs.

Q2 2025 Results

Comstock averaged 1,233 MMCFE per day in Q2 2025 production, which was towards the low-end of its guidance for 1,225 to 1,325 MMCFE per day in average production during the quarter. Comstock's capex was within expectations for the quarter, while its per unit operating costs were near the low-end of its guidance.

Weaker realized prices (as well as slightly higher capex) during Q2 2025 (compared to Q1 2025) contributed to a $79 million free cash deficit from operations during the quarter. This was around zero for Q1 2025.

Guidance Revision

Comstock made a noticeable negative revision to its guidance for 2025. It now expects 1,225 to 1,325 MMCFE per day in average production during the year, which (at guidance midpoint) is a 6% reduction from its prior guidance. Comstock's D&C capex budget was unchanged, while it added $10 million to its acreage budget for the year.

Thus we may have a situation (at guidance midpoint) where Comstock sees a 12% year-over-year decline in production despite spending around $1 billion on D&C capex.

Comstock continues to de-risk its Western Haynesville acreage, but has added back a legacy Haynesville rig in an attempt to reverse some of those production declines.

While it has made significant progress with de-risking the Western Haynesville and becoming more capital efficient there, its legacy Haynesville acreage still appears to offer better returns at this stage. As well, there have been some midstream issues as it builds out its Western Haynesville infrastructure, and that may slightly delay some wells being turned online.

2H 2025 Outlook

Comstock may average around 1,294 MMCFE per day during the second half of the year, based on its revised guidance midpoint. This would be a 3% increase from its 1H 2025 average production.

The NYMEX natural gas strip for 2H 2025 is now around $3.40, and Comstock is projected to generate $763 million in 2H 2025 revenues after hedges.
